02 · Descriptive & Prescriptive Analytics · 2025
Data Analyst Job Market Analysis
Labour Market Domain · Multi-Source · 17,076 Postings
Overview

A multi-source job postings analysis answering: what does the U.S. data analyst job market actually look like, and what does that mean for a candidate deciding where to invest their effort? Built from 17,076 post-deduplication postings scraped on March 28, 2026 across 25 U.S. metros from three independent sources — Indeed, LinkedIn, and Adzuna — enriched with BLS OES May 2024 occupational wage data.

Built as a governance-first pipeline: every claim is cross-validated, every recommendation is bounded to what the data structurally supports, and a formal refused recommendations section documents what was declined and why. Where the map runs out, the notebook names the edge rather than drawing past it.

Seven Confirmed Claims
Claim 1 — Compensation stratified across two independent levers: geography and industry
High

San Francisco leads at p50 $140,500 — 35% above the market-wide median of $104,000. A $58,000+ gap separates Information ($137,600) from Educational Services (~$79,000). The upper quartile clears six figures in every sector — p75 exceeds $100K across all 19 covered NAICS sectors.

Claim 2 — Three skills are the non-negotiable entry requirement; one more extends reach into the largest segment
High

Excel (63.85%), SQL (49.83%), and Python (31.08%) define Tier 1. Adding Tableau (26.60%) or Power BI (25.14%) is the one non-negotiable Tier 2 addition — it expands into BI Visualisation, the second-largest archetype at 28.74%.

Claim 3 — Skill category determines investment strategy, not frequency
High

A cloud platform at 8% and a BI tool at 8% represent categorically different decisions. Cloud platforms are stack-fit skills employers teach; BI tools are requirements candidates are expected to arrive with. Reading frequency as investment priority produces a candidate optimising against the wrong signal.

Claim 4 — General analyst is the correct entry target
High

General analyst (49.83%) and BI Visualisation (28.74%) define the broad centre. ML adjacent (6.52%) and modern data stack (3.25%) are real but materially smaller lanes. Tier 1 plus a visualisation tool is sufficient. Specialisation is a path from inside the field, not a pre-entry commitment.

Claim 5 — Sector shapes which archetype a posting requires
Medium · LinkedIn-only

IT Services leads governance signal at 27.1%. Software Development leads ML adjacent at 25.9%. Professional Services shows highest governance concentration at 64.3%, but across only 28 postings — a different proposition than lower concentration in a high-volume sector.

Claim 6 — The market is structurally less transparent for early-career candidates
High (structure)

Fewer than 100 combined postings disclose salary at the entry level. Posted salary figures skew toward mid-market and senior roles — the entry-level floor is directional, not reliable.

Claim 7 — Generative AI tool literacy is below ML signal today and formalisation is early
Low · Single snapshot

6.31% of postings mention a generative AI term vs. ML adjacent at 9.62%. Present but not saturated. A candidate who builds AI tool familiarity now enters ahead of formalisation — but this is a directional observation from one snapshot.

Five Recommendations
  • Target the general analyst archetype as the entry point. 49.83% of postings. Highest volume, lowest barrier with Tier 1 plus a visualisation tool.
  • Prioritise sector and geography simultaneously. IT Services and Software for technology-adjacent roles. Information sector for compensation ceiling. High-BLS-median metros for maximum range.
  • Build Tier 1 to interview-ready depth, add one visualisation tool, stop there. Everything else in Tier 2 is learnable on the job.
  • Treat every undisclosed salary posting as a negotiation opportunity. Direct employer conversation and competing offers are more reliable than any floor estimate this analysis can produce.
  • Build AI tool literacy now. The formative period of a skill transition is the one moment a less-experienced candidate has a structural advantage over a more-experienced one who has not yet adapted.
What Was Formally Refused
  • Career progression map refused. Dataset contains no longitudinal career history. Posting volume for adjacent titles says nothing about transition timelines or compensation step-changes.
  • Specific Tier 2 skill beyond visualisation tool refused. Cloud platforms and governance tools are learnable on the job. A second mandatory recommendation would contradict the category framework.
  • Specialisation as entry strategy refused. ML adjacent (6.52%) and modern data stack (3.25%) increase search difficulty from outside the field without compensating advantage.
  • Sector findings elevated to High confidence refused. LinkedIn-only population (17% of dataset). Indeed company_industry carries 76.7% nulls; Adzuna has no industry field.
